feedback.text: Hello, Digital ownership has become a real concern, and nowadays, many companies try to misuse the lack of protection by taking away right to own what we pay for. In games industry, this is on display with live service games, that get shut down and cut off from players, with no other alternative provided. It is akin to buying a car, having the car manufacturer shut down a production car, and confiscating your car along with that. This concern appears in other industries too, such as software products with lifetime licenses being made inaccessible to users, or subscription services tied to physical products that block previously paid for software features previously. I would like to ask you to include protection of digital ownership of videogames (and even other digital products), and introduce protections against digital/game publishers removing access to content (games) we paid for. If the company decides to kill of a service, they should provide a way for users to use this service in the future. As is well proven by existence of player hosted live service games, such as "private" World of Warcraft servers, customers can provide these services on their own. A common excuse used are the security concerns. These have proven to be nothing more then excuses, as the publishers and service providers have faced data leaks and security breaches again and again. On the other hand, ability to self host these games after their end of life greatly eliminates such issues. After all, instead of a single company maintaining thousands of users, you end up with great number of smaller self hosted services. And unlike these companies, users will not need to provide private credit card information or their real world addresses. In short, please consider adding protection of video games, mainly live service videogames, to the Digital Fairness Act.
